macro_segments.title: Androgynus and the Status of Teruma: Rabbi Yochanan vs. Resh Lakish on De-Oraita

micro_segments.title: Opening discussion: Androgynus can feed his wife Teruma and Matnot Kehuna
Rabbi Yochanan holds Teruma bizman hazeh is De-Oraita; Resh Lakish disagrees
Baraita of mixed baskets: two baskets of Teruma and Chulin with Seah that fell into them
Resh Lakish's position: requires majority of Chulin to permit; Rabbi Yochanan is more lenient
Gemara challenges Rabbi Yochanan: if Teruma is De-Oraita, how can it be Batel be-Rov?

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yose's Position on Teruma De-Oraita Established via Seder Olam

micro_segments.title: Gemara resolves: the Baraita reflects the position of Rabbanan (those who disagree with Rabbi Yose)
Rabbi Yose holds Teruma is De-Oraita, citing Seder Olam: first and second Yerusha maintain Kedusha
Scholarly discussion of Seder Olam as historical chronicle; nature as early rabbinic compilation
Explanation of Seder Olam: Galut Bavel eliminated Kedusha; Ezra re-established it; Galut Titus does not eliminate it

macro_segments.title: Exception for Derabbanan: When Rov Suffices Without Majority (Mikveh Case)

micro_segments.title: Gemara asks: how can Rabbi Yochanan permit Teruma as De-Oraita without Rov requirement like in Baraita?
Mikveh case: drawn water (Mayim She'uvim) is De-Rabbanan; Rov suffices to permit up to half mixture
Tosafot kashya: if fruit juice in Mikveh is De-Oraita, why does Rov work?
Answer: distinction between Rov for De-Rabbanan vs. Safek on status; reliance on possibility ('Shani Omer')

macro_segments.title: Androgynus Status: Male or Safek? Testing the Mishna Language

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Androgynus Noseh (marries) but Lo Nisha (is not wedded); Gemara tests if Noseh is Lechatchila or Di'avad
Possible reading: Noseh is Lechatchila, Lo Nisha is Di'avad; alternative: both are Di'avad
Discussion of Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Eliezer on Androgynus: Sekila penalty as evidence of male status
Consensus: Androgynus is male; dispute is whether Sekila applies from two places (different sexual acts)

macro_segments.title: Androgynus as Third Category: Rav's Challenge to the Mishna with a Baraita

micro_segments.title: Rav brings Baraita: Rabbi Yose says Androgynus is Briya be-Fnei Atzmo, not male or female
Gemara: both Mishna and Baraita have equal standing; why prefer Mishna over Baraita?
Scholarly context: Mishna as canonized oral text; Baraita as equal but unincluded source; role of Tosefta
Inference from Mishna: Rabbi Yose spoke with Rabbi Shimon in Mishna, now speaks alone in Baraita; did he change his mind?

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Rule: When to Prefer Mishna over Baraita on Contradictory Positions

micro_segments.title: Shmuel: Mishna has authority over Baraita when they contradict; Gemara cites his general rule about Yachida
Qualification: Shmuel's stringency for Yachida (individual opinion) applies only when Yachida is in Mishna

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Rule: When to Prefer Mishna over Baraita on Contradictory Positions (continued)

micro_segments.title: Halacha: follow Rabbi Yose on Androgynus and Grafting (Harkava)

macro_segments.title: Halacha According to Rabbi Yose: Androgynus, Grafting, Difficult Birth, and Mixed Field

micro_segments.title: School of Rav says Halacha is like Rabbi Yose on Androgynus and Harkava (grafting)
Shmuel says Halacha is like Rabbi Yose on Koshi (difficult birth) and Kidush (mixed field)

macro_segments.title: Grafting: Timing Requirements Before Shmitta Year

micro_segments.title: Mishna: cannot plant, clear land, or graft less than 30 days before Rosh Hashanah in Shmitta year
Dispute: Rabbi Yehuda says graft takes 3 days; Rabbi Yose and Rabbi Shimon say 2 weeks
Rav Nachman: add the takeover period to 30-day limit (3 or 30 days, or 14 or 30 days)

macro_segments.title: Difficult Birth (Koshi): Blood During Pregnancy and Childbirth Complications

micro_segments.title: Definition: woman seeing blood outside her normal menstrual time; if continuous 3 days, she is Zava
Pregnant woman in labor: blood from contractions not counted as Zivah, even if continuous
Dispute on limit: Rabbi Meir says 40 or 50 days; Rabbi Yehuda says Rosh Chodesh; Rabbi Yose and Rabbi Shimon say 2 weeks
